parlay_results.parlay_summary: 1/5 clean hit on the Volume Game Triple — three of the remaining four parlays were neutralized by Holmgren and Embiid DNPs (PARTIAL), with the one true miss driven by the Murray 3PM goose egg in the Mobley-Murray Floor Lock.
parlay_lessons: Three of five parlays went PARTIAL due to Chet Holmgren's DNP appearing as a leg in two parlays (Kawhi-SGA Feeder Stack, Kawhi-Jokic-Holmgren Sweeper) and Embiid's DNP appearing in a third (Jokic-Embiid Dual Big) — when multiple players in the parlay pool share a high lineup_risk flag (OUT pre-game), the structural probability of at least one parlay going PARTIAL approaches near-certainty; the Parlay Agent should treat any OUT-flagged player as ineligible for parlay inclusion regardless of prop quality, and should not construct two or more parlays sharing the same high-risk leg.

The Mobley-Murray Floor Lock missed because the Murray 3PM leg (8/10, up trend, T1) produced a zero — 3PM T1 legs are structurally fragile as parlay components because a single cold-shooting game eliminates an entire card despite other legs hitting comfortably; future parlays should avoid 3PM legs entirely unless the player has an iron-floor tag and trend=stable or up with a minimum 9/10 hit rate, and even then should be treated as the weakest leg in any combination.

The session's only clean parlay HIT (Volume Game Triple: Kawhi REB, SGA PTS, Mobley REB) succeeded by combining two different REB floor props with one iron-floor PTS prop across three independent games — this structure avoided 3PM legs entirely and avoided any player with a lineup risk flag, which was the decisive structural advantage over the four parlays that went PARTIAL or MISS.
parlay_reinforcements: The Volume Game Triple's REB + PTS structure across three independent games with no teammate overlap and no high-lineup-risk players was the cleanest combination on the card — pairing two iron-floor REB props (Kawhi T4, Mobley T2) with one iron-floor PTS prop (SGA T25) across different games and teams produced a comfortable clean sweep; this REB-REB-PTS trileg structure on independent games with 78%+ confidence floors should be a go-to parlay template.

The feeder-target correlation logic (SGA AST → Holmgren PTS within the same OKC-DEN game) was directionally sound — SGA did deliver 15 assists confirming the feeder role — but the correlated leg structure was rendered moot by Holmgren's DNP; when building intra-game correlated legs, both players must have confirmed active status at parlay lock time, not just at the pre-game injury check window, making same-game correlated parlays higher operational risk than cross-game independent structures.

reinforcements: Low-threshold PTS floors (T10 and T15) on high-usage primaries with 9/10 or 10/10 hit rates proved near-automatic: Randle T10 (hit 14), RJ Barrett T10 (hit 25), Duren T15 (hit 26), Brandon Miller T15 (hit 23) — these tiers are the system's most reliable prop class.

Multi-stat coverage on elite scorers paid off cleanly: Durant went 3/3 (PTS/REB/AST), Doncic went 3/3 (PTS/REB/3PM), and Derrick White went 3/3 (PTS/REB/AST) — stacking confirmed players at their floor tiers across prop types is high-value.

The 3PM T1 floor (OVER 1) strategy on high-volume shooters was 5/5 when restricted to consistent-tagged players with 9/10 or 10/10 rates (Doncic, Booker, Reaves, Cunningham, Knueppel) — this tier is a systematic edge.
REB picks at T4 and T6 with confirmed L10 floor-gate passage went 8/8 across all players — the 25th-percentile 3rd-lowest gate is functioning correctly as a filter against volatility.

The blowout-resilient PTS classification correctly protected Duren (+14.5 underdog, hit 26) and Cunningham (+14.5 underdog, hit 15 AST) — the primary scorer exemption from blowout secondary-skip is validated.
lessons: Jaylen Brown's iron-floor PTS tag failed badly (8 actual vs 20 pick) in just 15 minutes — the post-game context shows only 15 minutes played, suggesting an early exit or coach decision. The iron-floor tag and 8/10 hit rate did not protect against a minutes collapse. When min_floor is derived from full-game samples, a 15-minute game is structurally unforeseeable, but the miss reveals that iron-floor confidence capping at 80% may be appropriate even for consistent-tagged scorers when no minute-protection signal exists.

Brandon Ingram's PTS miss (9 actual vs 15 pick, VOLATILE tag, 7/10 hit rate) in 36 minutes confirms that the VOLATILE penalty at 70-72% confidence is the correct zone for him — the reasoning cited vs_soft=85% as a supportive signal, but 7/10 at T15 for a volatile SF is a marginal pick even in favorable matchups. The Analyst should treat 7/10 VOLATILE PTS picks at T15+ as borderline-skip candidates regardless of DvP rating.

RJ Barrett's AST miss (1 actual vs 2 pick, iron-floor tag, 8/10 hit rate) in 32 minutes with a down trend is a low-tier variance miss — the player logged 32 minutes and 25 points but only 1 assist. The down trend was correctly noted but the iron-floor tag suppressed the VOLATILE penalty. Iron-floor classification should not override a down-trend + declining AST pattern for wing scorers; the trend signal deserved more weight.

Julius Randle's AST miss (2 actual vs 4 pick, 7/10 hit rate, VOLATILE) confirms that T4 AST picks for non-primary PGs remain risky at 70% confidence — the reasoning correctly flagged VOLATILE -5% bringing confidence to 70%, and the pick still missed. The system is right to be cautious here; this classification validates the 70% floor threshold as a borderline zone.

Cooper Flagg's PTS miss (14 actual vs 15 pick, iron-floor tag, 7/10 rate) with only 1 point below threshold illustrates that T15 iron-floor picks at exactly 70% confidence with FG_COLD:-18% present are genuinely marginal. The FG_COLD signal was logged as 'informational only' but a -18% shooting suppression on a 15-point threshold is a material risk that should trigger a tier step-down to T10.

LaMelo Ball's PTS miss (14 actual vs 15 pick, 9/10 rate, 22 minutes played) — the post-game context shows only 22 minutes, well below his typical load. A min_floor=22.9 below 24 triggered a confidence cap but did not trigger a tier step-down. The minutes-floor cap rule should be evaluated for whether it's doing enough: a min_floor below 24 should perhaps force a mandatory tier step-down to T10 rather than just capping confidence.
recommendations: Institute a mandatory tier step-down for PTS picks where min_floor < 24 AND the pick tier is T15 or higher: LaMelo Ball (min_floor=22.9, T15 missed by 1) and Flagg (FG_COLD -18%, T15 missed by 1) both fell just below threshold — dropping to T10 in these cases costs little upside and eliminates the near-miss exposure. Apply this as a rule: min_floor < 24 on a T15 PTS pick → step to T10.

Treat FG_COLD >= -15% as a hard tier-reduction signal, not informational: Flagg's FG_COLD was -18% and his PTS pick was T15 at 70% confidence. The informational-only designation allowed an analytically weak pick to proceed. Recommendation: FG_COLD <= -15% should force a one-tier step-down on PTS picks, matching the existing FG_MARGIN_THIN rule logic.

For wing scorers (SG/SF) with a down trend on AST, do not apply iron-floor classification to suppress the VOLATILE penalty: Barrett's AST miss (1 actual, down trend, 32 minutes, 25 points scored) shows that high scoring output does not guarantee assist accumulation. The down trend on AST for wings should trigger a VOLATILE deduction even when the iron-floor tag is present — iron-floor should protect the tier pick, not the confidence level.

Add a minutes-exit risk modifier for players whose L10 log contains any game with fewer than 20 minutes played in a non-DNP context: Brown played only 15 minutes despite no pre-game flag. If any L10 game shows <20 active minutes (not a DNP), apply a -5% confidence penalty and note as MINUTES_RISK. This is a distinct signal from min_floor and would have reduced Brown's PTS confidence below the 80% assigned.

For 3PM picks classified as VOLATILE with a tough DvP, maintain the existing approach of not applying additional tough-DvP penalties per the 3PM noise rule — the Edwards 3PM miss (1 actual, 7/10 rate, tough DvP) is a clean variance outcome on a 70% confidence pick. The rule is correct; the Edwards miss is within expected 30% failure rate and does not warrant rule changes.

miss_details.root_cause: 7/10 hit rate with VOLATILE -5% landing at 70% confidence — this is the system's defined margin zone. The analyst correctly applied the T3+ ceiling rule (7/10 AND up trend), noted tough DvP with no additional penalty per 3PM noise rule, and landed at 70%. Edwards logged 31 minutes and had an off shooting night (1 3PM). At 70% confidence, a 30% miss rate is expected; this is a clean variance outcome within the model's stated uncertainty. The tier walk correctly identified T4 as blocked and stepped to T3, and the pick was appropriately marginal. No rule or signal failure.

Brown logged only 15 minutes per post-game context — an early exit or coach-managed minutes restriction that was not predictable from any pre-game signal (injury_status_at_check = NOT_LISTED, no voiding). The iron-floor tag and 8/10 hit rate (with min_floor=26.1 >= 24) all indicated a sound pick. The reasoning cited 'mid SA defense won't suppress Brown's volume' which was correct in isolation, but the miss was entirely driven by a minutes collapse below any floor the model tracks. This is a model_gap_signal: NBAgent has no mechanism to detect in-game minutes management decisions by coaching staff that occur without a pre-game injury flag. The post-game context confirms 15 minutes played with no injury confirmation, suggesting a load-management or game-script decision. Note: this was the parlay-breaking leg in 'Iron Floor Quad.'

Barrett logged 32 minutes and scored 25 points — fully active with substantial usage — but accumulated only 1 assist against a 2-pick threshold. The iron-floor tag and 8/10 hit rate at T2 supported the pick, and the down trend was correctly acknowledged with a conservative 78% confidence cap. The analyst's reasoning ('reliable secondary playmaker accumulating assists at T2') was reasonable for a wing scorer. However, in games where Barrett concentrates on scoring (25 PTS), assist accumulation can collapse to near-zero — this is within the 22% miss rate implied by 78% confidence. Classified as variance. Note: the down trend on AST was a legitimate caution signal; the iron-floor tag appropriately prevented a VOLATILE deduction but the down trend deserved slightly more weight in the confidence calculation.

Randle logged 26 minutes and scored 14 points but recorded only 2 assists vs the 4-pick threshold. The analyst correctly applied VOLATILE -5% to reach 70% confidence and noted the borderline hard-gate condition (PF position, AST avg ~4.7, soft DvP). The 7/10 hit rate in a VOLATILE context at 70% confidence is the system's defined edge zone — a 30% miss rate is expected. The reasoning ('playmaking role confirmed with primary ball-handlers available') is correct in principle but the soft DvP context did not translate to assist volume in this specific game. Clean variance at the model's stated 70% confidence floor.

Ball logged only 22 minutes — well below his typical load — and scored 14, missing by exactly 1 point. The pick had min_floor=22.9 < 24, which correctly triggered a confidence cap, but the rule only capped confidence rather than forcing a tier step-down. With min_floor below 24 and a T15 PTS pick, the existing rule structure was insufficient: Ball's reduced minutes (22 logged) were the proximate cause of missing the threshold. A min_floor < 24 should mandate a tier step-down to T10 on PTS picks of T15 or higher — the signal existed in the quant data at pick time (min_floor=22.9 was computed and present) but the rule only adjusted confidence, not the tier. This is a model_gap_rule miss: the signal was available; the rule didn't fully act on it.

Flagg logged 32 minutes and scored 14 — missing by exactly 1 point with a FG_COLD:-18% signal logged as 'informational only.' The iron-floor tag and 7/10 hit rate placed this at the system's minimum viable threshold (70%), but the FG_COLD -18% signal was explicitly present in the quant data at pick time and not acted upon as a tier modifier. The analyst's reasoning correctly identified Flagg as DAL's primary scorer with boosted usage, but the FG_COLD signal was material: a shooter 18% below baseline shooting efficiency on a T15 PTS pick is a meaningful suppression risk. The rule treating FG_COLD as informational-only allowed this marginal pick to proceed at T15 when T10 would have been the appropriate tier. Model_gap_rule: the FG_COLD signal existed, the rule did not use it to step down the tier.

Ingram logged 36 minutes — fully active — but scored only 9 points, well below the 15-pick threshold. The VOLATILE tag and 7/10 hit rate correctly placed this at 72% confidence, the system's acknowledged margin zone for volatile scorers. The analyst's reasoning cited vs_soft=85% as supportive, which is a real signal, but the 7/10 rate combined with VOLATILE is the system's weakest qualifying combination for a T15 PTS pick. At 72% confidence, a 28% miss rate is expected, and Ingram's volatile shooting profile means misses can be significant in magnitude (not near-misses). The pick was technically sound per system rules but borderline — Ingram's VOLATILE + 7/10 + T15 profile is a candidate for future skip-list consideration without additional confluence signals.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: 1/2 parlay session — 'Scorers Sweep' hit cleanly on all four legs while 'Iron Floor Quad' was broken by Jaylen Brown's unforeseeable 15-minute exit, making Brown the single point of failure on an otherwise 3/4 card.
parlay_lessons: The 'Iron Floor Quad' miss exposes a structural concentration risk in parlays that include any player with min_floor < 24 or an undocumented minutes-management history: Brown's 15-minute exit was not predictable from any pre-game signal, but parlays cannot absorb even one minutes-collapse leg — future parlays should explicitly screen out any leg where min_floor < 24 or where the player has shown any in-season load-management pattern, regardless of iron-floor classification.

Including a 20-point PTS threshold leg (Brown OVER 20) in a 4-leg parlay alongside T10 floor legs (Barrett OVER 10, Randle OVER 10) creates asymmetric risk: if the high-threshold leg is the anchor of the parlay narrative ('Brown's up-trend vs soft SA'), the parlay's structural integrity depends on a leg that is materially harder to hit than its partners. The parlay agent should cap the highest single-leg threshold in any multi-leg card to no more than 1.5x the median threshold of all legs (here, median was 12.5, so max should be ~19 — Brown at 20 was marginally above this).
parlay_reinforcements: The 'Scorers Sweep' structure — stacking primary scorer PTS legs from independent games where all players have soft or mid DvP and 8/10+ hit rates at their selected tier — produced a clean 4/4 sweep (Miller 23, Durant 29, Giannis 22, Randle 14 all cleared). This multi-game independent scorer stack with matched floor thresholds (T15/T20/T20/T10) and no cross-game correlations is the system's most validated parlay template.

Low-threshold anchor legs (T10 PTS at 10/10 hit rate) serve as structural insurance in multi-leg parlays: Randle OVER 10 (hit 14) and Barrett OVER 10 (hit 25) both cleared comfortably and appeared in both parlays. These legs should always be included when available as a near-certain base in any parlay construction, as they reduce the probability of a full miss even when a higher-threshold leg underperforms.

reinforcements: Low-threshold PTS floors (T10-T15) on volume scorers with 9-10/10 hit rates proved nearly automatic: Harden 30, Edwards 36, Kawhi 45, Banchero 25, LaMelo 30, Knueppel 24 all cleared comfortably — the tier-walk step-down discipline is working precisely as designed.

AST props at conservative thresholds (T2-T6) went a perfect 10/10, confirming that pairing a soft-to-mid AST DvP gate with a player's documented iron floor or 10/10 hit rate is a high-confidence structure regardless of trend direction.

3PM props at T1 with 9-10/10 hit rates swept 6/6 even through a trend=down case (Bane) and a trend=down with iron_floor override (Mitchell) — the step-down-to-T1 rule on down-trending shooters is well-calibrated.

The BLOWOUT_RISK -10% confidence cap (Knueppel, LaMelo, both at 74%) correctly restrained overconfidence on a 12.5-point spread game; both players still hit comfortably, validating that the blowout_games resilience counter (93-95%) can support including these picks at reduced confidence rather than skipping them.

The tough DvP hard gate on AST (Randle, Kawhi) correctly stepped down from T4 to T2 in both cases, producing clean 10/10 floors — the hard gate rule on position-specific tough DvP AST is preventing overreach and should be maintained.
lessons: Blowout game script is a scoring suppressor even for elite centers: Jokic missed PTS 20 with 16 actual in a 36-point DEN blowout win — his triple-double (13 AST, 12 REB) confirms he played but scored less as the game became garbage time. The reasoning noted 'spread=6.5 competitive enough to protect minutes' but a 36-point final margin was not captured pre-game; the system needs a real-time blowout game script adjustment, not just a pre-game spread gate.

Kevin Durant's PTS miss (11 actual vs. 20 threshold, 26 minutes played) on a 9/10 hit rate is the most concerning miss: the reasoning cited '8-game hit streak' and 'min_floor=32.5 above 24-min threshold' but he logged only 26 minutes — suggesting either a load management decision or an early exit that was not injury-classified. With no post-game news confirming injury and no pre-game OUT flag, the 6-minute shortfall from the stated min_floor is unexplained and represents a model gap around minutes floor reliability in away games against specific opponents.

Sengun REB missed badly (2 actual vs. 6 threshold) despite a 10/10 hit rate, 80% confidence, and a tough DvP. With 24 minutes played, he was active and contributing. A single catastrophic REB game (2) against a tough opponent on a B2B road game suggests that the 10/10 hit rate at T6 was masking a tail risk: the L10 3rd-lowest REB=6 floor cited in reasoning was exactly at the threshold, leaving zero cushion. When the 3rd-lowest L10 value equals the pick threshold exactly, there is no protective buffer — one bad game breaks the streak.
recommendations: Implement a live game script adjustment for PTS props on centers and high-usage forwards: when the in-game spread expands beyond 20 points, apply a scoring suppression flag. The Jokic miss (16 PTS in a 36-point blowout) and the reasoning's reliance on pre-game spread (6.5) illustrate that pre-game spread alone is insufficient — build a second-half load management discount into the model for marquee big men in non-competitive games.

Add a minutes floor reliability check for away games: Durant's stated min_floor of 32.5 was not met (26 min actual). When a player's away B2B minutes history shows variance >5 min from the stated floor, downgrade confidence by 5% or flag the pick for manual review. The 'min_floor' field should be sourced from away-game-only splits, not overall averages, to avoid overestimating floor reliability on road legs.

Tighten the REB floor gate: when the L10 3rd-lowest value equals the pick threshold exactly (as with Sengun REB=6 gating at T6), require a 1-unit cushion — i.e., the 3rd-lowest L10 value must be strictly greater than the pick threshold, not equal to it. An exact match provides no variance buffer and a single outlier game causes a miss. Apply this rule to all prop types.

For Jokic PTS specifically: given his triple-double profile and Denver's tendency to run up scores against weak opponents, consider a conditional rule that steps down Jokic PTS by one tier (T25→T20→skip) when the opponent is ranked bottom-5 defensively AND DEN is a home favorite by 6+. The blowout risk is structurally higher for Jokic than for guards because his scoring is more discretionary in garbage time.

Validate Kevin Durant's miss classification next session with additional data: his 26-minute appearance against a stated min_floor of 32.5 is either a load management decision (model_gap_signal — no quant field captures coach rest decisions mid-game) or an undisclosed minor injury exit. If confirmed as load management, add a 'star load management away B2B' penalty flag for veterans on road second nights — even those with 100% B2B resilience ratings.

miss_details.root_cause: Sengun played 24 minutes and recorded stats (PTS=10 confirmed HIT), so this is not a DNP or injury exit — he was active. The tier_walk shows REB: 8→5/10 | 6→10/10✓, with the 10/10 hit rate at T6 fully supporting the pick. The reasoning cited 'L10 3rd-lowest REB=6 passes 25th-pct gate at T6' — but this is precisely the vulnerability: the floor was set exactly at the threshold with zero cushion. A 2-rebound game on 24 minutes in a road B2B against a tough DEN opponent represents a tail-end outlier event on an otherwise near-perfect recent track record. No pre-game signal existed to flag this; the pick was structurally sound. However, the exact-threshold floor (3rd-lowest L10 = pick value) is a pattern worth tightening in the selection rule. Classified as variance: the 10/10 hit rate fully supported the pick; one catastrophic outlier game in 10 is within the expected variance envelope for this threshold.

Durant played 26 minutes and recorded supporting stats (REB=4 HIT, AST=5 HIT), confirming he was active and not injured. The tier_walk shows PTS: 25→4/10 | 20→9/10✓, and the reasoning cited a 9/10 hit rate, an 8-game streak, and a min_floor of 32.5 above the 24-minute threshold. The 6-minute shortfall from the stated min_floor (26 actual vs. 32.5 projected) is the proximate cause of the scoring miss — 11 points in 26 minutes is plausible production at that minutes level. No pre-game OUT or DOUBTFUL flag existed; injury_status_at_check = NOT_LISTED. The system had no mechanism to anticipate a mid-game minutes restriction that was not injury-driven. This is a model_gap_signal: NBAgent does not currently compute a 'coach-imposed minutes cap on away B2B for veteran stars' signal, and no quant field in the pick object would have flagged this. The stated min_floor reliability in away games and the absence of a load management probability field are the structural gaps.

Jokic played 30 minutes and posted a triple-double (13 AST HIT, 12 REB implied by the AST HIT and web recap), confirming full activity. The post-game recap confirms: 'Denver's 129-93 blowout win over Houston... lopsided nature of the game likely limited his scoring opportunities in the second half.' The tier_walk shows PTS: 25→5/10 | 20→10/10✓, and the reasoning cited 'spread=6.5 competitive enough to protect minutes.' However, the pre-game spread was 6.5 in DEN's favor — a meaningful home favorite projection — and the final margin was 36 points. The reasoning's reliance on a 6.5-point spread as a 'competitive' threshold was too permissive for a center whose scoring is discretionary in blowout conditions. A signal existed in the pick object (spread=6.5, DEN home) that a tighter rule could have caught: centers and primary big men on home favorites by 6+ against bottom-tier offenses (HOU mid DvP) carry elevated blowout scoring suppression risk. The model has a BLOWOUT_RISK penalty for large spreads (≥12) but not for moderate spreads that still produce lopsided games. This is a model_gap_rule: the signal (moderate spread on a dominant home center) existed in context but the prompt rule's blowout penalty threshold was not tuned to catch this scenario.

parlay_results.parlay_summary: Clean 2/2 sweep — both multi-game parlays anchored by low-threshold PTS iron floors across independent games, with every leg clearing by a wide margin.
parlay_lessons: Both parlays shared the same two anchor legs (RJ Barrett PTS 10, Alperen Sengun PTS 10), creating hidden single-game concentration risk: if Sengun had missed his PTS prop (he hit exactly at threshold with 10.0), both parlays would have busted simultaneously. The Parlay Agent should track anchor leg overlap across same-session parlays and flag when the same leg appears in multiple cards — even on a winning day, this structural concentration means a single player's bad game wipes out the entire parlay card, not just one bet.

The Sengun PTS leg hit exactly at threshold (actual=10.0, pick=10) — a razor-thin margin that anchored both parlays. Given Sengun's same-game REB miss (2 actual) and reduced minutes (24), his overall output was suppressed in this game. Parlays using a player as an anchor should require their floor prop to have at least a 1-unit buffer above threshold in the L10 3rd-lowest, not just an exact-match floor, to reduce the risk of a parlay-busting near-miss.
parlay_reinforcements: The multi-game independent-scorer structure (three players from three different games, each with 9-10/10 hit rates at conservative PTS thresholds) is the optimal parlay architecture: no shared game script risk, no correlated blowout exposure, and each leg independently justified. This structure produced 6/6 legs hit across both parlays and should be the primary template for future multi-game cards.

Stacking a trending scorer (Banchero, up trend, 10/10, soft DvP) with two iron-floor anchors (Barrett T10, Sengun T10) is a high-EV parlay structure: the trending scorer provides the upside asymmetry (Banchero hit 25 vs. 15 threshold) while the anchors provide near-certain floor coverage. This 'upside leg + two floors' construction is worth formalizing as a named parlay template.

reinforcements: Low-threshold AST props (T2/T4) on primary ball-handlers delivered a 92.9% hit rate — the single best prop type of the session. Picks like LeBron AST T4 (7 actual), SGA AST T4 (9 actual), and Reaves AST T2 (7 actual) all cleared by wide margins. The conservative threshold strategy on AST is working precisely as designed.

3PM T1 props on volume shooters in favorable matchups hit 7 of 8 qualifying picks (excluding voided White). Doncic T3 (9 actual), Booker T1 (4 actual), Murray T1 (2 actual), and Reaves T1 (4 actual) all cleared. The T1 floor combined with high pace/soft DvP matchups provides a robust structural edge.

The BLOWOUT_RISK + spread≥12 confidence cap (hard cap at 74%) correctly constrained picks in LAL vs CHI (spread ~12) without blocking valid selections. Players like Luka (51 PTS), Reaves (30 PTS), and LeBron (18 PTS) all hit comfortably, validating that the cap managed overconfidence without generating false negatives.

Iron_floor picks in competitive games hit 100% of the time this session — Chet Holmgren PTS T10 (14 actual), Cooper Flagg REB T6 (6 actual, exact threshold = HIT), Jaylen Brown AST T2 (7 actual). The iron_floor tag is providing reliable signal when game script is not adversarial.

Applying mandatory tier step-downs triggered by min_floor < 24 (e.g. Jaylen Brown PTS T20→T15, Jalen Duren PTS T15→T10) produced hits in both cases, confirming the rule's conservative function is correct — the step-down protected against marginal tiers without sacrificing the pick entirely.
lessons: Bam Adebayo 3PM T1 missed (0 actual vs. pick 1): The 'up trend' and 9/10 hit rate created overconfidence in a low-frequency event prop for a center. At T1 3PM for a non-perimeter player, a single 0-for-3PM game is always in-range variance, but the signal that Adebayo is a C with relatively low 3PM volume makes this structurally fragile regardless of recent trend. The trend=up tag on 3PM for big men needs additional scrutiny.

Cade Cunningham 3PM T2 missed (0 actual vs. pick 2): The tier_walk explicitly noted VOLATILE-5% would drop confidence to 65% and flagged a SKIP — yet the pick appeared in the graded set at 70% confidence. This is a possible workflow enforcement gap: if VOLATILE-5% is correctly reducing T2 3PM to 65% below the 70% floor, the pick should have been suppressed entirely before publication.

Desmond Bane REB T2 missed (1 actual vs. pick 2): The 25th-pct gate showed 3rd-lowest L10 REB = 3 > 2, which appeared safe. However, in a game that finished ORL +5 (CLOSE), Bane played 42 minutes — a usage pattern suggesting heavy offensive load but limited rebounding opportunity at the wing position. The REB miss here reflects that wing/guard REB T2 props are vulnerable to game-flow effects that push the player toward ball-handling rather than crashing the glass. The 9/10 overall rate may overstate reliability in close games where wings operate in transition-heavy modes.

Jalen Duren PTS T15 missed at 14 actual (one point below threshold): After the mandatory min_floor step T15→T10 was applied in the tier_walk, the pick was published at T10 per the reasoning — yet the graded pick_value shows 15, not 10. This is a pick recording inconsistency: the tier_walk logic stepped down to T10 but the published pick_value remained T15. The actual result of 14 would have been a HIT at T10. This is a model_gap_rule failure in pick publication — the stepped-down tier must be reflected in the final pick_value field.
recommendations: Enforce a categorical rule: for C and PF players (Adebayo, Jokic, Duren, Holmgren, etc.), 3PM T1 picks require a minimum of 7/10 hit rate AND the player's 3PM average across L10 must exceed 0.8 per game. A trend=up tag alone is insufficient to qualify a low-volume 3PM prop for a big man — add an explicit L10 3PM average floor gate before publishing these picks.

Fix the tier step-down publication pipeline: when a mandatory step-down is applied (e.g. min_floor < 24 triggers T15→T10), the final pick_value field in the published pick must reflect the stepped-down tier (T10 = pick_value: 10), not the original evaluated tier. The Duren PTS case (tier_walk said T10, pick_value published as 15, actual was 14) would have been a HIT at T10. Implement a post-walk pick_value reconciliation check before publication.

Add a volatility flag specifically for wing/guard REB T2 props in CLOSE games (spread_abs < 6): when a wing player (SG/SF/PF hybrid) has a REB T2 pick and the pre-game spread is under 6 points, apply an additional -5% confidence deduction. Close games push wings into playmaking roles rather than rebounding, and the Bane REB miss (1 actual, ORL +5 final) illustrates this dynamic. The 9/10 overall rate masks game-script fragility at this tier.

Establish a VOLATILE + SKIP enforcement audit: any pick where the tier_walk explicitly concludes 'SKIP' due to VOLATILE-5% dropping below 70% must not appear in the published graded picks above 70% confidence. The Cunningham 3PM T2 pick showed 70% confidence in the graded record despite the tier_walk documenting a SKIP decision. Add a post-processing validation step that cross-checks tier_walk SKIP conclusions against published confidence values and suppresses any pick where SKIP was reached.

For 3PM props on players with trend=down, apply the existing hard skip rule consistently even at T1 — the Payton Pritchard 3PM T1 pick was internally flagged as hard-skip (trend=down + tough DvP) but published at 74% and hit. While this was a beneficial outcome, the inconsistency between the tier_walk logic and the published pick creates calibration noise. Either enforce the hard skip universally or document explicitly when iron_floor overrides the skip, so the rule is deterministic rather than ad hoc.

STEP 4 — Hit rate was 9/10 (90%) at pick time with trend=up; this is a legitimate variance miss on a high-confidence pick, not a systemic error. STEP 5 — Tier walk showed T1 was the only qualifying tier after T2 fell below 70%; the pick was at the lowest viable tier. STEP 6 — No amendment object present. The structural concern is that 3PM props for C-position players have inherently higher single-game variance than PTS/REB props — the overall 85% rate may overstate game-to-game reliability at T1 for big men.

Additionally, the BLOWOUT game (22-point DET win) compressing Duren's minutes to 15 is consistent with starters being rested in Q4 of a blowout, further supporting that the T15 threshold was too aggressive for this game script even before the min_floor rule. parlay_results.parlay_summary: 2/4 parlay session — both misses were caused by the single Derrick White DNP, which torpedoed two parlays simultaneously, while the two hits were driven by independent multi-game low-threshold legs that had no shared game-script exposure.
parlay_lessons: A single player appearing as an anchor leg in multiple parlays creates correlated parlay failure risk even when the games are nominally independent: Derrick White's DNP killed both 'Thursday Floor Sweep' and 'Bam-White Cross-Game Lock' simultaneously, turning one availability event into a 2-parlay loss. Cap any single player to one parlay leg per session to prevent this concentration failure.

Parlays that include a player with QUESTIONABLE or elevated injury_status_at_check should apply a structural downgrade — even if the pick itself clears confidence thresholds, the parlay as a unit becomes fragile. The pre-game OUT status on White was known at pick time (injury_status_at_check = OUT), meaning both parlays containing him should have been voided or reconstructed before lock. Implement a parlay-level sweep that voids any parlay containing a leg where the underlying pick was subsequently voided.

The 'Bam-White Cross-Game Lock' parlay (parlay_04) lost 2 of 3 legs — White DNP and Bane REB miss — leaving only the Adebayo REB hit. The label 'Cross-Game Lock' implied high confidence across independent games, but combining an injury-risk scorer with a wing REB low-threshold prop created two independent fragility points. Parlay construction should favor legs from the same prop-type strength category (AST and 3PM T1 are performing above 85%+) rather than mixing PTS anchor with REB floor props that have different variance profiles.
parlay_reinforcements: The 'Multi-Game Volume Triple' and 'Independent Scorers Sweeper' both hit cleanly by anchoring on ultra-low AST thresholds (T2 at 2 assists) combined with a high-confidence PTS or scoring prop. The AST T2 leg type — characterized by 9/10 hit rate, iron_floor tags, and soft DvP — is the most structurally reliable parlay building block in the current system, providing near-certain floor coverage while the anchor scorer leg provides upside.

Cross-game parlays with zero lineup overlap and no shared game-script exposure (Reaves/LAL, Giannis/MIL, SGA/OKC — three entirely different games) demonstrated clean independence: when one leg outperforms dramatically (Giannis 31 vs. 20 needed, SGA 35 vs. 25 needed), the cushion absorbs any single-leg near-miss. Prioritize parlays where at least two legs are from games with spread_abs < 10 to minimize blowout game-script interference across the entire ticket.
